---
title: "Geospatial for Engineers"
subtitle: >-
  Earth observation and geospatial data, explained for experienced software
  engineers with zero domain background.
---

## Who this is for

You already write software. You are comfortable with JSON, the command line,
data structures, and reading an unfamiliar API. What you *don't* have is a
geospatial or earth-observation (EO) background: terms like CRS, projection,
raster, coverage, footprint, COG, and Zarr go by without quite landing, and most
tutorials assume the domain knowledge you're missing while carefully explaining
the code you already understand.

This series inverts that assumption. It spends its words on the **domain** --
what these concepts *mean* and *why they exist* -- and trusts you with the code.
It is the on-ramp I wanted when I started doing EO work without the background.

## How it's organized

- **Foundations** -- the core geospatial ideas the rest depends on (coordinate
  reference systems, projections, raster vs. vector, coverages, footprints).
- **Data formats** -- the file and interchange formats you'll actually meet,
  one self-contained guide each (CoverageJSON, Zarr, and more to come).
- **Libraries & tools** (planned) -- practical, runnable introductions to the
  Python geospatial stack, once the foundations and formats are in place.

Each guide stands on its own, but if you're starting cold, read **Foundations**
first -- it's the spine the others hang from.

## Status

This is an actively growing, work-in-progress personal learning project. Guides
marked *planned* are stubs; expect rough edges and frequent change.

::: {.callout-note}
The first complete guide is the [CoverageJSON guide](formats/coveragejson.qmd).
It doubles as the template for the series: patient, example-driven, and
diagram-forward.
:::
